title: A habit you wish your city had
summary: Argue that your city should adopt a specific habit or practice from another place, explaining the benefits and addressing likely objections.
topic: A habit you wish your city had
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) arguing that your city should adopt a specific habit or practice that works well somewhere else in the world. Give a concrete example of the habit in action, explain its benefits, and address at least one objection. Use present perfect, modal verbs, and at least one concession with 'although' or 'however'.
hintStarters: Of all the habits I have noticed in other cities, the one I keep returning to is…
The city I grew up in has always been good at many things, but it has never quite managed…
Although changing a city's habits is never simple, the evidence from places like… suggests that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: khiến việc… trở nên dễ dàng hơn
một mô hình đã được chứng minh
cái giá của sự thụ động
định hình lại cuộc sống hàng ngày
một can thiệp chi phí thấp

sampleText:
Of all the habits I have noticed in other cities, the one I keep returning to is the tradition of open street markets on weekend mornings. I grew up in Bogotá, where Sunday ciclovías have been a part of city life for decades — miles of roads are closed to cars so that cyclists, joggers, and families can take up space that normally belongs to traffic. I have seen something similar in Auckland and in Amsterdam, but the version I wish my own current home, Lagos, would adopt is simpler: a weekly pedestrian market in a few central streets, where residents sell food, plants, and handmade goods to their neighbours.

Although Lagos already has informal street markets, they are often squeezed onto narrow pavements and treated as obstacles rather than as assets. A properly organised weekend market would do something different. It would slow the pace down in a city that rarely pauses. It would foster community between people who live near each other but have no regular reason to meet. It has been shown in cities from Seoul to Accra that these kinds of low-cost interventions reshape daily life in ways that parks and official events do not, because they are run by the residents themselves rather than imposed from above. The market becomes a reason to walk, to linger, and to recognise a face.

The most common objection is traffic. Closing a street, even briefly, means redirecting cars, and in a city with Lagos's density, that can cause real disruption. However, this objection misunderstands the scale being proposed. A single street closed for four hours on a Sunday morning is a manageable experiment, not a permanent overhaul of the road network. Cities that have piloted the idea — including Nairobi and Buenos Aires — have found that the benefits to local business, air quality, and social cohesion outweigh the inconvenience. If Lagos started with one street in one neighbourhood, the model could scale up wherever residents wanted it. The cost of inaction is a city that continues to exist at speed, without a single habit that slows it down long enough for people to actually meet.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the one I keep returning to
take up space that normally belongs to traffic
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: chiếm không gian thường thuộc về giao thông

sampleAnnotations.phrase: treated as obstacles rather than as assets
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: bị đối xử như trở ngại thay vì tài sản
các can thiệp chi phí thấp
định hình lại cuộc sống hàng ngày

sampleAnnotations.phrase: a reason to walk, to linger
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: lý do để đi bộ, để nán lại
hiểu nhầm quy mô được đề xuất
một thí nghiệm có thể quản lý được

sampleAnnotations.phrase: scale up wherever residents wanted it
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: mở rộng quy mô bất cứ nơi nào cư dân muốn
cái giá của sự thụ động

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for ongoing relevance
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Sunday ciclovías have been a part of city life for decades' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để nhấn mạnh rằng thực hành này bắt đầu trong quá khứ nhưng vẫn tiếp tục đến ngày nay. Dùng 'for + khoảng thời gian' với hiện tại hoàn thành.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Sunday ciclovías have been a part of city life for decades' uses the present perfect to stress that this practice began in the past and continues now. Use 'for + time period' with the present perfect.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession with 'although'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Although Lagos already has informal street markets, they are often squeezed onto narrow pavements' thừa nhận thực tế hiện có (thị trường tồn tại) trước khi lập luận rằng cần cải thiện — cấu trúc nhượng bộ điển hình ở B1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Although Lagos already has informal street markets, they are often squeezed onto narrow pavements' acknowledges an existing reality (markets exist) before arguing for improvement — a typical B1 concession structure.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong tất cả những thói quen tôi đã quan sát ở các thành phố khác, điều tôi cứ quay lại là truyền thống chợ đường phố mở cửa vào buổi sáng cuối tuần. Tôi lớn lên ở Bogotá, nơi ciclovías Chủ Nhật đã là một phần của cuộc sống đô thị trong nhiều thập kỷ — hàng dặm đường được đóng cửa với xe hơi để người đi xe đạp, chạy bộ và các gia đình có thể chiếm không gian thường thuộc về giao thông. Tôi đã thấy điều tương tự ở Auckland và Amsterdam, nhưng phiên bản tôi ước ngôi nhà hiện tại của mình, Lagos, sẽ áp dụng thì đơn giản hơn: một khu chợ đi bộ hàng tuần ở một vài con phố trung tâm, nơi cư dân bán thực phẩm, cây cảnh và hàng thủ công cho hàng xóm của họ.

Mặc dù Lagos đã có chợ đường phố không chính thức, chúng thường bị chen chúc vào những vỉa hè hẹp và bị đối xử như trở ngại thay vì tài sản. Một khu chợ cuối tuần được tổ chức đúng đắn sẽ làm điều gì đó khác biệt. Nó sẽ làm chậm nhịp sống lại trong một thành phố hiếm khi dừng lại. Nó sẽ xây dựng cộng đồng giữa những người sống gần nhau nhưng không có lý do thường xuyên để gặp gỡ. Người ta đã chứng minh ở các thành phố từ Seoul đến Accra rằng những can thiệp chi phí thấp như thế này định hình lại cuộc sống hàng ngày theo những cách mà công viên và sự kiện chính thức không làm được, bởi vì chúng do chính cư dân điều hành thay vì bị áp đặt từ trên xuống. Khu chợ trở thành lý do để đi bộ, để nán lại và để nhận ra một khuôn mặt.

Phản đối phổ biến nhất là giao thông. Đóng cửa một con phố, dù chỉ trong thời gian ngắn, có nghĩa là phải chuyển hướng xe hơi, và trong một thành phố với mật độ dân số của Lagos, điều đó có thể gây ra sự gián đoạn thực sự. Tuy nhiên, sự phản đối này hiểu nhầm quy mô được đề xuất. Một con phố duy nhất đóng cửa trong bốn giờ vào sáng Chủ Nhật là một thí nghiệm có thể quản lý được, không phải là sự tái cấu trúc vĩnh viễn mạng lưới đường bộ. Những thành phố đã thử nghiệm ý tưởng này — bao gồm Nairobi và Buenos Aires — nhận thấy rằng lợi ích đối với doanh nghiệp địa phương, chất lượng không khí và sự gắn kết xã hội vượt trội hơn sự bất tiện. Nếu Lagos bắt đầu với một con phố ở một khu dân cư, mô hình có thể được mở rộng bất cứ nơi nào cư dân muốn. Cái giá của sự thụ động là một thành phố tiếp tục tồn tại trong tốc độ, không có một thói quen nào làm chậm lại đủ lâu để mọi người thực sự gặp nhau.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'have been' + noun phrase + 'for' correctly?
options: Ciclovías have been a part of city life since decades.
Ciclovías has been a part of city life for decades.
Ciclovías have been a part of city life for decades.
Ciclovías have being a part of city life for decades.
mcQuestions.explanation: Plural subject takes 'have been'; 'for' + period of time (not 'since' + point in time).
mcQuestions.hint: 'For' + period of time; 'since' + specific point in time.

options: Although Lagos already has informal street markets, but they are often treated as obstacles.
Lagos already has informal street markets, although because they are often treated as obstacles.
Although, Lagos already has informal street markets, they are often treated as obstacles.
Although Lagos already has informal street markets, they are often treated as obstacles.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no comma after 'although' and no extra conjunction.
mcQuestions.hint: No comma after 'although'; no 'but' or 'so' before the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a modal to express a hypothetical result?
options: If Lagos started with one street, the model could scale up wherever residents wanted it.
If Lagos starts with one street, the model could scale up wherever residents wanted it.
If Lagos started with one street, the model will scale up wherever residents want it.
If Lagos started with one street, the model could scaled up wherever residents wanted it.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: 'if + past simple, modal + bare infinitive' for a hypothetical present scenario.
mcQuestions.hint: Second conditional: 'if + past simple' in the condition, 'could/would + base verb' in the result.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'however' correctly to introduce a counter-argument?
options: However this objection misunderstands the scale being proposed.
However, this objection misunderstands the scale being proposed.
This objection, however misunderstands the scale being proposed.
This objection misunderstands however the scale being proposed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'However' at the start of a sentence is followed immediately by a comma.
mcQuestions.hint: 'However' as a discourse connector at the sentence start always needs a comma after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses the passive voice with present perfect?
options: It has showed in cities from Seoul to Accra that these interventions reshape daily life.
It have been shown in cities from Seoul to Accra that these interventions reshape daily life.
It has been shown in cities from Seoul to Accra that these interventions reshape daily life.
It has been show in cities from Seoul to Accra that these interventions reshape daily life.
mcQuestions.explanation: Passive present perfect: 'it has been shown' — singular 'it' takes 'has'; past participle of 'show' is 'shown'.
mcQuestions.hint: Passive present perfect = 'has/have been + past participle'. 'Show' → 'shown'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly uses 'rather than' to contrast two things?
options: Markets should be treated as assets rather than obstacles being.
Markets should be treated as assets, rather as obstacles.
Markets should be treated as assets rather as than obstacles.
Markets should be treated as assets rather than as obstacles.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Rather than' is followed by the same grammatical structure on both sides: 'as assets' / 'as obstacles'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Rather than' needs parallel structure on both sides.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'would' correctly for a future hypothetical result?
options: A properly organised market would slow the pace down and foster community.
A properly organised market will slow the pace down and foster community.
A properly organised market would slows the pace down and foster community.
A properly organised market would to slow the pace down and foster community.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would + bare infinitive' expresses a hypothetical result; bare infinitive has no 's' or 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'would', always use the bare infinitive (no 's', no 'to').

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'whereas' to contrast two different cities?
options: Whereas Nairobi has piloted street markets successfully but many other cities have been slower.
Whereas Nairobi has piloted street markets successfully, many other cities have been slower to act.
Nairobi has piloted street markets successfully whereas, many other cities have been slower.
Whereas, Nairobi has piloted street markets successfully, many other cities have been slower.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whereas + clause, main clause' — no comma after 'whereas' and no extra conjunction like 'but'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Whereas' at the start of a clause is not followed by a comma, and the main clause needs no 'but'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which relative clause is formed correctly?
options: A city which it continues to exist at speed, without a habit that slows it down.
A city that continues to exist at speed, without a habit which slows down it.
A city that continues to exist at speed, without a habit that slows it down.
A city continues to exist at speed, without a habit that slows down it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'That continues' (subject relative) and 'that slows it down' (with correct word order) are both right.
mcQuestions.hint: In a relative clause, don't repeat the subject ('which it') and keep verb + object order ('slows it down').

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses parallel structure in a list?
options: It would slow the pace down, fostering community, and give residents a reason to meet.
It would slow the pace down, foster community, and giving residents a reason to meet.
It would slowing the pace down, foster community, and give residents a reason to meet.
It would slow the pace down, foster community, and give residents a reason to meet.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'would', all verbs in a list must be bare infinitives: 'slow … foster … give'.
mcQuestions.hint: After a modal, maintain parallel form throughout the list: bare infinitive for each item.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly expresses a benefit using 'outweigh'?
options: The benefits to local business and social cohesion outweigh the inconvenience.
The benefits to local business and social cohesion outweighs the inconvenience.
The benefits to local business and social cohesion are outweigh the inconvenience.
The benefits to local business and social cohesion outweighed the inconvenience always.
mcQuestions.explanation: Plural subject 'the benefits' takes plural verb 'outweigh' in the present simple.
mcQuestions.hint: Check subject–verb agreement: plural subject 'benefits' takes 'outweigh' (no -s).

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is it possible to transplant a habit from one city to another, or do habits only work in the culture that created them? Think of an example where a city borrowed a practice from elsewhere and it either worked or failed.
